Layout.metadataTitle: MigraineCast — Predict Migraines Before They Strike
Layout.metadataDescription: MigraineCast helps you spot migraine patterns linked to weather, pressure, and triggers — so attacks don't come out of nowhere.
Layout.organizationDescription: MigraineCast helps migraine sufferers predict and prepare for attacks by tracking patterns linked to weather, barometric pressure, and personal triggers.
Layout.websiteDescription: Predict migraines before they strike with MigraineCast. Track patterns, understand weather triggers, and take control.

SpecialistFinder.ogTitle: Find a Migraine Specialist Near You
SpecialistFinder.heroDescription: Search for headache and migraine specialists and neurologists in your area on an interactive map — no signup required.
SpecialistFinder.searchCardDescription: Enter your city or zip code, or use your current location.
SpecialistFinder.searchPlaceholder: Search for a city (e.g. London, New York…)

SpecialistFinder.resultsDisclaimer: Results are pulled from Google Maps and may include general practitioners and clinics in addition to headache specialists. Always verify a provider's credentials and that they're accepting new patients before booking.
SpecialistFinder.ctaTitle: While you wait for an appointment, track your triggers.
SpecialistFinder.ctaDescription: MigraineCast tracks barometric pressure trends for your location and gives you advance warning before weather-related attacks — useful data to bring to your next appointment.

faqs.question: What kind of doctor treats migraines?
faqs.answer: Neurologists are the specialists most commonly trained to diagnose and treat migraines, particularly those with a headache medicine subspecialty. Primary care doctors can also manage migraines, but a neurologist or headache specialist is usually the right referral for frequent, severe, or hard-to-control attacks.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist for migraines?
faqs.answer: Consider seeing a specialist if you're having migraines more than a few times a month, if over-the-counter medication isn't helping, if your headaches are getting worse or changing in character, or if you experience visual aura, numbness, or other neurological symptoms for the first time.

faqs.question: What's the difference between a neurologist and a headache specialist?
faqs.answer: A neurologist treats conditions of the brain and nervous system broadly, including migraines. A headache specialist is a neurologist (or sometimes another type of physician) who has completed additional fellowship training specifically in headache medicine, focusing on migraine, cluster headache, and other chronic headache disorders.

faqs.question: How do I prepare for my first appointment with a migraine specialist?
faqs.answer: Bring a record of your attack frequency, duration, and typical symptoms, along with any medications you've tried and how well they worked. Noting potential triggers — including weather, sleep, diet, and stress patterns — gives the specialist a much clearer picture to work from. Tools like MigraineCast's Trigger Pattern Analyzer can help you organize this history before your visit.

faqs.question: Will my insurance cover a visit to a headache specialist?
faqs.answer: Most insurance plans cover visits to neurologists and headache specialists, though many require a referral from your primary care doctor first. Coverage details vary by plan, so it's worth calling ahead to confirm referral requirements and whether a specific provider is in-network.
